ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken rice dish class; no specific entry for honey-glazed chicken rice with fried egg. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for honey chicken rice with fried egg. A0CKM (rice-based dish with poultry) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for this composite dish. Omitted to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set plate of honey chicken rice with fried egg, approximately 380g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice, honey-glazed chicken pieces, and one fried egg.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Chicken Rice With Fried Egg Set — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Chicken Rice With Fried Egg Set (cr_id G00010305), confidence 0.95, provenance canonical.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey chicken rice with fried egg as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white jasmine rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008). The honey glaze and fried egg contribute negligible additional carbohydrate relative to the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 57.5g carbohydrate in 380g serving) / 100 ≈ 41.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324mg/100g with low potassium offset (182mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (1.0g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its elevated sodium content of 324mg/100g; if consumed, request reduced-sodium sauces, sauce on the side, and a poached or boiled egg instead of fried to reduce total sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with moderate carbohydrate load; honey glaze adds additional free sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should moderate portion size due to the high-GI white rice base and honey glaze; substituting brown rice, reducing the rice portion, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 324mg/100g, estimated saturated fat ~1.2g/100g from fried egg and chicken skin, and cholesterol 13mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly due to its elevated sodium and saturated fat from the fried egg and honey-glazed chicken; requesting a poached egg, skinless chicken, and reduced-sodium sauces will meaningfully lower the cardiovascular risk profile of this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Chicken Rice with Fried Egg Set (蜜汁鸡肉饭配煎蛋套餐) is a popular Singaporean hawker and food-court set meal consisting of steamed white jasmine rice topped with honey-glazed chicken pieces and accompanied by one pan-fried egg. The chicken is typically marinated and cooked with honey, soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and sesame oil, producing a sweet-savoury glaze with a slightly caramelised exterior. The fried egg adds richness and additional protein to the meal. Per 100g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96g protein, 4.0g fat, and 15.12g carbohydrate, with sodium at 324mg — elevated relative to hypertension thresholds. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is widely available across Singapore's hawker centres and food courts, commonly served at lunch and dinner. It is halal-friendly and dairy-free but contains egg, soy, gluten (from oyster sauce), and sesame allergens. Clinically, the primary concerns are sodium load for hypertension patients and high glycaemic load for diabetic individuals; the fried egg contributes additional saturated fat and AGEs. Requesting brown rice, a poached egg, and reduced-sodium sauces substantially improves the nutritional profile of this meal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010305) with confidence 0.95 and provenance marked canonical. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the reported panel is not available in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish as of 2026. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: no trans fats expected in this dish based on typical preparation with vegetable cooking oil and no hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per HPB source context. saturated_fat_g of 1.2g/100g is estimated from the fried egg and chicken components using USDA FoodData Central analogous values (fried egg ~3.3g sat fat/100g; chicken with skin ~2.5g sat fat/100g) weighted by approximate ingredient proportions; this is an estimation and should be updated if HPB publishes a detailed fatty acid panel. The input cultural.primary_cuisine was listed as 'Thai' but the dish is a Singaporean hawker staple sold across Singapore food courts; cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean' based on HPB provenance and regional context. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
